title: Advanced Prompting Capstone
concept: Assembling persona, chaining, and self-consistency into a single robust prompting strategy; 'Next up: Context Engineering.'.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why can a prompt that worked perfectly last month suddenly start giving worse answers today, even though you never touched it?

options.text: The underlying model was silently upgraded, and the prompt wasn't re-tested on the new version
The user's internet connection changed the model's behavior

exercises.explain: As covered in the last lesson, model providers regularly upgrade their models behind the scenes. A prompt tuned for one version can silently regress on a newer version, which is why re-testing prompts after upgrades is essential.

cards.title: Time to Build the Whole House
cards.body: You've learned the bricks — persona, chaining, self-consistency — separately. But a real production prompt needs all of them working together, like a house needs a foundation, walls, AND a roof, not just one part.

cards.title: The Three Pillars Combined
cards.body: A robust prompting strategy layers three things: PERSONA (who the AI acts as, setting tone and expertise), CHAINING (breaking one big task into ordered sub-steps), and SELF-CONSISTENCY (running key steps multiple times and picking the most common/reliable answer). Together they make outputs more accurate, consistent, and trustworthy.

cards.title: Think Movie Production
cards.body: Persona is the actor you cast, chaining is the shooting schedule (scene by scene), and self-consistency is filming multiple takes and picking the best one. A great movie needs all three working together, not just one.

cards.title: Building a Financial Advisor Bot
cards.body: Step 1 (Persona): 'You are a cautious, certified financial advisor who explains risk clearly.' Step 2 (Chain): First summarize the user's goals, then list options, then recommend one with reasoning. Step 3 (Self-Consistency): Run the 'recommend one' step 5 times and only show the recommendation if at least 3 of 5 runs agree.

cards.title: Myth: More Techniques = More Complexity Only
cards.body: Myth: stacking persona, chaining, and self-consistency just makes prompts slower and harder to maintain. Truth: when combined thoughtfully, they reduce errors so much that you often need FEWER retries and less manual fixing overall, saving time in production.

cards.title: Single Trick vs Full Strategy
cards.body: A prompt using just one technique is fragile under pressure. A prompt combining persona, chaining, and self-consistency handles edge cases far better.

left.text: Persona-only prompt sounds right but still makes silent factual slips on hard steps.

right.text: Persona sets tone, chaining structures the task, self-consistency catches the rare wrong answer before it ships.

exercises.prompt: Which combination best describes a robust capstone prompting strategy?

options.text: Persona + chained steps + self-consistency checks on critical steps
Random wording changes each time you run the prompt
Self-consistency only, with no defined role or steps

exercises.explain: The robust approach combines all three: a persona for consistent tone and expertise, chaining to break complexity into manageable steps, and self-consistency to catch errors on the steps that matter most.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Self-consistency is only useful when persona and chaining are absent from your prompt.
exercises.explain: False. Self-consistency works best ON TOP of persona and chaining — you run a well-structured, well-framed step multiple times to catch rare errors, not as a replacement for structure.

exercises.sentence: Breaking a big task into ordered sub-steps so the model handles one piece at a time is called chaining.
exercises.explain: Chaining means splitting a complex task into a sequence of smaller steps, which helps the model stay focused and reduces mistakes compared to asking for everything in one giant leap.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three prompt-strategy descriptions was likely generated by an AI trying to sound impressive but is actually vague and unhelpful?

options.text: 'Leverage synergistic paradigm optimization to holistically actualize maximum output excellence across all dimensions of engagement.'
'Set the persona as a math tutor, break the problem into 3 steps, then run the final answer 3 times and pick the majority result.'
'Ask the model to first list assumptions, then solve step by step, then double-check the final number against the original problem.'

exercises.explain: The first option is full of buzzwords ('synergistic paradigm optimization', 'holistically actualize') but says nothing concrete about persona, steps, or checks — a classic sign of AI-generated filler text. The other two give specific, actionable strategies.

exercises.prompt: You're building a prompt for an AI that gives medical study summaries to nursing students. It must sound like a calm expert nurse-educator, break down complex topics into steps, and avoid one-off mistakes on life-critical facts. Which strategy combo is most robust?

options.text: Use only a persona: 'You are a caring nurse-educator' and nothing else
Give the persona, chain the explanation into ordered steps, and run the fact-checking step multiple times to vote on the safest consistent answer
Skip persona and chaining, just ask the model to 'be accurate' once
Use self-consistency alone with no persona or steps, since voting fixes everything

exercises.explain: Robust prompting stacks the tools: persona sets tone and expertise framing, chaining breaks a complex task into manageable ordered steps (reducing errors from doing too much at once), and self-consistency runs the riskiest steps multiple times and keeps the answer that appears most often — catching rare mistakes before they reach the student. Relying on just one tool leaves gaps the others were built to cover. Next up: Context Engineering.
